chunks.text: More Dynamic Programming Examples Prof. Timothy Sun

Suppose there is an art gallery with n paintings in a row, and the ith painting is worth p[i] dollars. What’s the most value a burglar can steal, without stealing consecutive paintings? Art Thieves $12m $11m $1m $2m $9m $8m

Suppose there is an art gallery with n paintings in a row, and the ith painting is worth p[i] dollars. What’s the most value a burglar can steal, without stealing consecutive paintings? Art Thieves $12m $11m $1m $2m $9m $8m
Either you steal or don’t steal the ﬁrst painting. Art Thieves Recurrence
Either you steal or don’t steal the ﬁrst painting. ● If you don’t, you can still steal anything after it Art Thieves Recurrence

Either you steal or don’t steal the ﬁrst painting. ● If you don’t, you can still steal anything after it ● If you do, you get that painting, but you can’t steal the second painting Art Thieves Recurrence
M(i): most money for paintings i through n ● M(n+1) = 0 ● M(i) = max(M(i+1), p[i]+M(i+2)) Art Thieves Recurrence
M(i): most money for paintings i through n ● M(n+1) = M(n+2) = 0 ● M(i) = max(M(i+1), p[i]+M(i+2)) Careful what you recurse on! Art Thieves Recurrence
Art Thieves Backtracking
Art Thieves Dynamic Backtracking algorithm: Storing subproblems: Fill order:
Art Thieves Dynamic Backtracking algorithm: Storing subproblems: M[1, …, n+2] Fill order: decreasing
Art Thieves Dynamic
Art Thieves Dynamic Runtime: Θ(n)
Skateboarding Suppose jumping off each ramp keeps you in the air some distance. What’s the most number of jumps you can do?
Skateboarding Suppose jumping off each ramp keeps you in the air some distance. What’s the most number of jumps you can do? 3

Subsequences Unlike subarrays, subsequences don’t need to be contiguous: SALESMAN The string “SSMN” is a subsequence of “SALESMAN”

Subsequences Unlike subarrays, subsequences don’t need to be contiguous: SALESMAN The string “SSMN” is a subsequence of “SALESMAN” How many ways to select a subsequence from a string with n characters?

Subsequences Unlike subarrays, subsequences don’t need to be contiguous: SALESMAN The string “SSMN” is a subsequence of “SALESMAN” How many ways to select a subsequence from a string with n characters? 2n (think subsets)
Longest Common Subsequence Given two strings, what is their longest common subsequence? ABACUS BAUHAUS
Longest Common Subsequence A few more examples of strings and their LCSs: PARTY APTLY or PARTY APTLY ALGORITHM ALTRUISTIC CGATGATC TGGCATAC
Longest Common Subsequence Step 1: Design a backtracking algorithm (for size of LCS)
Longest Common Subsequence Step 1: Design a backtracking algorithm (for size of LCS) ● Subproblems: substrings
Longest Common Subsequence Step 1: Design a backtracking algorithm (for size of LCS) ● Subproblems: substrings What’s the “nibble”?
Longest Common Subsequence Step 1: Design a backtracking algorithm (for size of LCS) ● Subproblems: substrings What’s the “nibble”? ● Let’s see what we can do with the last characters of each string.
Longest Common Subsequence Let’s see what we can do with the last characters of each string. PRIESTS SPIRES Last chars are the same. Do they belong to every LCS?
Longest Common Subsequence Let’s see what we can do with the last characters of each string. PRIESTS SPIRES Last chars are the same. Do they belong to every LCS? No: PRIESTS SPIRES

Longest Common Subsequence Let’s see what we can do with the last characters of each string. PRIESTS SPIRES Last chars are the same. Do they belong to every LCS? No: PRIESTS SPIRES …but we can always modify an LCS to get one where both last chars belong.
Longest Common Subsequence Let’s see what we can do with the last characters of each string. PRIEST SPIRE Last chars are different. Do either belong to an LCS?
Longest Common Subsequence Let’s see what we can do with the last characters of each string. PRIEST SPIRE Last chars are different. Do either belong to an LCS? Maybe, but not both.

Longest Common Subsequence Let’s see what we can do with the last characters of each string. PRIEST SPIRE Last chars are different. Do either belong to an LCS? Maybe, but not both. Recurse on (PRIES, SPIRE) and (PRIEST, SPIR) and see which is bigger.
LCS Recurrence Given two strings S[1, …, m], T[1, …, n] L(i, j) = length of LCS of S[1, ..., i] and T[1, ..., j]
LCS Recurrence Given two strings S[1, …, m], T[1, …, n] L(i, j) = length of LCS of S[1, ..., i] and T[1, ..., j] To ﬁnd: base cases, recurrence
LCS Recurrence Base cases: S = empty string, T = anything (or vice versa) ●
LCS Recurrence Base cases: S = empty string, T = anything (or vice versa) ● L(0, j) = L(i, 0) = 0
LCS Recurrence If last characters match, remove both and recurse on the rest ● L(i, j) = If last characters don’t match, take the better of two one-char deletions ● L(i, j) =

LCS Recurrence If last characters match, remove both and recurse on the rest ● L(i, j) = 1 + L(i-1, j-1) If last characters don’t match, take the better of two one-char deletions ● L(i, j) = max(L(i-1, j), L(i, j-1))
The ﬁnal recurrence: LCS Recurrence
LCS Backtracking
LCS Dynamic Programming Step 1: Design a backtracking alg. Step 2: Store the subproblems
LCS Dynamic Programming Step 1: Design a backtracking alg. Step 2: Store the subproblems - 2D array L[0, …, m][0, …, n]
LCS Dynamic Programming Step 1: Design a backtracking alg. Step 2: Store the subproblems - 2D array L[0, …, m][0, …, n] Step 3: Fill order - ???

LCS Reconstruction ● If there’s a match, step diagonally
LCS Reconstruction ● If there’s a match, step diagonally ● Otherwise, move up, then left (in same layer) until you ﬁnd another
LCS Reconstruction ● If there’s a match, step diagonally ● Otherwise, move up, then left (in same layer) until you ﬁnd another
● If there’s a match, step diagonally ● Otherwise, move up, then left (in same layer) until you ﬁnd another LCS Reconstruction
● If there’s a match, step diagonally ● Otherwise, move up, then left (in same layer) until you ﬁnd another LCS Reconstruction
● If there’s a match, step diagonally ● Otherwise, move up, then left (in same layer) until you ﬁnd another LCS Reconstruction
● If there’s a match, step diagonally ● Otherwise, move up, then left (in same layer) until you ﬁnd another LCS Reconstruction (one possible) LCS: TAN
Does this process ever get stuck? LCS Reconstruction
Does this process ever get stuck? This situation is impossible. LCS Reconstruction
LCS Reconstruction
Edit Distance How quickly can you change “ALERT” into “LATER”, with the following operations? ● Inserting a character ● Deleting a character ● Substituting a character

Edit Distance How quickly can you change “ALERT” into “LATER”, with the following operations? ● Inserting a character ● Deleting a character ● Substituting a character 3 operations: ● ALERT → ALER (deletion) → LALER (insertion) → LATER (substitution)
Edit Distance Both LCS and edit distance can be thought of as “alignment problems”:
Edit Distance Both LCS and edit distance can be thought of as “alignment problems”: Edit distance has a similar solution (what happens for those last characters?)
ED Recurrence Base cases: S = empty string, T = anything (or vice versa) ●
ED Recurrence Base cases: S = empty string, T = anything (or vice versa) ● E(0, j) = j, E(i, 0) = i
ED Recurrence When both strings are non-empty, take the best operation: ● E(i, j) = min(insertion, deletion, substitution)
ED Recurrence When both strings are non-empty, take the best operation: ● E(i, j) = min(insertion, deletion, substitution) ○ Insertion = E(i, j-1) + 1 ○ Deletion = E(i-1, j) + 1

ED Recurrence When both strings are non-empty, take the best operation: ● E(i, j) = min(insertion, deletion, substitution) ○ Insertion = E(i, j-1) + 1 ○ Deletion = E(i-1, j) + 1 ○ Substitution = E(i-1, j-1) + 1

ED Recurrence When both strings are non-empty, take the best operation: ● E(i, j) = min(insertion, deletion, substitution) ○ Insertion = E(i, j-1) + 1 ○ Deletion = E(i-1, j) + 1 ○ Substitution = ■ E(i-1, j-1) + 1 if S[i] ≠ T[j] ■ E(i-1, j-1) if S[i] = T[j] (if the characters match, no cost)
ED Recurrence The ﬁnal recurrence:

ED Dynamic Programming The “shape” of base cases and dependencies is exactly the same as LCS
ED Dynamic Programming The “shape” of base cases and dependencies is exactly the same as LCS ● Let’s reuse the same ﬁll order
ED Dynamic Programming
ED Dynamic Programming Runtime: Θ(mn)
Recursive deﬁnition for winning If the game has already been won/lost, nothing to calculate. A state is winning if there is a move the player can make that makes it losing for the other player.
Nim - variant 1 First version: 1 pile, 1-3 stones per turn, person who takes last loses
Nim 1 - game trees
Nim 1 - DP solver
Nim 1 Actually there’s a really simple strategy:
Nim - variant 2 Second version: ● 2 piles ● any (positive) # of stones from 1 pile ● player who takes last stone wins
